oxford.title: Oxford Summer School
oxford.subtitle: Knowledge Representation, Aug. 2018
oxford.summary: A short academic branch at Oriel College that widened the undergraduate horizon before the later research-heavy years.

blocks.text: The old resume lists this as a summer school in knowledge representation at Oriel College, Oxford.

items: Oriel College
Knowledge representation
A brief international academic detour

hust.title: HUST Electronic and Information Engineering
hust.subtitle: Bachelor's degree, 2016-2020
hust.summary: The undergraduate base where software, computer vision, modeling, and student-organization work first started to braid together.

blocks.text: I graduated in the top 10 with a 3.91/4.0 GPA. Research, contests, design work, and course projects gradually pulled the path toward machine learning and software.

items: Electronic and Information Engineering
Research and capstone project work
Student associations and design work

cornell.title: Cornell M.Eng. in ECE
cornell.subtitle: Graduate study in Ithaca
cornell.summary: The Cornell period connected data visualization, machine learning, systems, and the path from internship to full-time work at Verily.

blocks.text: Coursework and projects stretched across visualization, vision, and systems. Life in Ithaca also opened into travel and, later, work in California.

items: INFO 5100 data visualization projects
CS 6670 and ECE project work
Bridge into the Verily internship

signature-attack.title: Signature Verification Attack
signature-attack.subtitle: Black-box adversarial ML research
signature-attack.summary: A research thread on region-restricted black-box attacks against handwritten signature verification that later became a Pattern Recognition paper.

blocks.text: The work started from a course interest in adversarial examples. It focused on keeping perturbations inside the stroke region, so they stayed hard to notice and survived background cleaning.

items: Started in 2019 in Wei Yuan's lab
Published in Pattern Recognition, 2021
Region-restricted perturbations for cleaner attacks

noise-modulation.title: Noise Modulation
noise-modulation.subtitle: Interpretability and robustness study
noise-modulation.summary: An unpublished paper proposing a lightweight way to recover cleaner, more interpretable input gradients without full adversarial training.

blocks.text: The idea was to multiply images with learned noise during training. That pushed the model to rely on the truly critical components, cleaning up the gradient signal at the cost of some accuracy.

items: Worked on with Xinggang Wang
Cheap alternative to one aspect of adversarial training
Interesting result, but not publishable in its current form

wellmail.title: WELLMAIL
wellmail.subtitle: Python e-mail client
wellmail.summary: A first serious software project: a Python e-mail client with a GUI, built as a team course project and remembered as an early step into real software construction.

blocks.text: The main lesson was not only the client itself. It was learning how to divide a graphical software system into modules and build something with a real interface flow.

items: Receive Box and Main Module ownership
Course project for Software Project
One of the strongest projects in the class cohort

louvre-evacuation.title: Louvre Evacuation Model
louvre-evacuation.subtitle: ICM 2019 modeling project
louvre-evacuation.summary: A contest modeling project that used a cellular-automata-style abstraction of the Louvre to design a dynamic evacuation strategy.

blocks.text: Instead of modeling each person as a cell, the system modeled representative exhibition halls. It then simulated how congestion-aware routing could accelerate evacuation.

items: Interdisciplinary Contest in Modeling, 2019
Dynamic congestion-control plan
Honorable Mention

weibe.title: WEIBE
weibe.subtitle: Weibo emotion analysis system
weibe.summary: A course-built system that crawled Weibo trending topics, stored posts, and visualized emotional tendencies across those conversations.

blocks.text: The main contribution was system design: a Selenium crawler, MongoDB-backed storage, Python server work, and a simple frontend for exploring the results.

items: Capstone Project in Big Data Processing System
Python, Flask, Gevent, MongoDB
Captured topics during the early COVID-19 moment

faceswap.title: FaceSwap Deepfake System
faceswap.subtitle: Undergraduate capstone project
faceswap.summary: A bachelor's graduation project focused on generating face-swapped short videos, with attention to the full pipeline rather than only the core model.

blocks.text: The core work centered on the intracoder and color-correction modules. The broader challenge was stitching detection, alignment, fusion, and post-processing into one pipeline.

items: Generation-focused deepfake pipeline
Identity swap plus color correction modules
Detection treated as a survey in the final paper

online-kitchen.title: Online Kitchen
online-kitchen.subtitle: Interactive visualization project
online-kitchen.summary: A Cornell data visualization project built with D3.js around food, nutrition, and production stories.

blocks.text: This sat in the Cornell cluster where interface storytelling and data structure started to feel central rather than peripheral.

items: INFO 5100 course project
Collaborative Cornell build
Interactive web visualization

programming-languages.title: Programming Languages Visualization
programming-languages.subtitle: Interactive D3 project
programming-languages.summary: A Cornell visualization piece exploring programming-language history, family relationships, and popularity through interactive graphics.

blocks.text: The project combined history, relation maps, and trend views. In hindsight, it feels especially close to the graph-based personal-site idea.

items: Language family network
Popularity and speed views
Collaborative Cornell course project

naec.title: Research Engineer Intern at NAEC
naec.subtitle: National Anti-counterfeit Engineering Research Center
naec.summary: A part-time research internship where a course interest in adversarial examples became sustained work on handwritten signature verification and, later, a journal publication.

blocks.text: This was the period when adversarial machine learning stopped being just a course topic and became a real research track. Working with Prof. Wei Yuan's group, I focused on attacking signature verification systems under realistic black-box constraints.

items: Part-time internship during the HUST years
Worked on handwritten signature verification attacks
Fed directly into the RSAEG / region-restricted signature paper

hust-mclab.title: Research Assistant at HUST Media and Communication Lab
hust-mclab.subtitle: Adversarial ML with Prof. Xinggang Wang
hust-mclab.summary: A research-assistant period focused on adversarial robustness, interpretability, and what adversarial training changes inside a model.

blocks.text: This lab period deepened the adversarial-ML thread that began with the signature project. Much of the reading, experimentation, and writing around noise modulation came together here.

items: Studied adversarial training and robustness literature in depth
Developed the noise modulation idea around interpretable input gradients
Balanced paper-writing ambitions with unresolved accuracy tradeoffs

realai.title: Software Algorithm Engineer Intern at RealAI
realai.subtitle: AI security internship in Beijing
realai.summary: A short industry chapter in AI security that sat between academic robustness research and later product engineering work.

blocks.text: RealAI felt like a bridge. The same adversarial-ML interests appeared in a more applied setting, shaped by industry pace and product constraints. It sharpened the sense that robustness ideas had to survive outside the paper world.

items: Worked as a software algorithm engineer intern
Stayed close to AI security and robustness themes
Helped mark the transition from research-heavy work toward engineering roles

verily-intern.title: Software Engineer Intern at Verily
verily-intern.subtitle: Summer 2022, from Cornell to California
verily-intern.summary: The internship that turned graduate school into a clearer engineering path, connecting Cornell coursework with California life and later full-time work.

image.alt: Haoyang in the Bay Area during the Verily internship summer
image.caption: Work, relocation, and weekend travel started to blur into one California chapter.

blocks.text: This internship marks a real shift in the story of the graph. It is less about papers and experiments, and more about software teams, production thinking, and the rhythm of engineering work in practice.

It also changed how the summer itself felt. Workdays at Verily and weekend drives like [California State Route 1](/nodes/california-one-2022) started to belong to the same chapter rather than separate categories.

items: Software engineering became a concrete path rather than an abstract possibility
The distance between coursework and real product work became much smaller
The internship later connected forward into the full-time role that follows in the graph

blocks.title: Practical effect
blocks.text: The important part was not just learning new tools. It was seeing how engineering decisions accumulate in a team setting and how much clarity can come from shipping something that other people actually depend on.

verily.title: Software Engineer at Verily
verily.subtitle: Full-time chapter beginning in 2023
verily.summary: The current long-running chapter: product engineering, system ownership, and the part of the graph that connects most directly back to the present-day bio node.

blocks.text: Compared with the earlier nodes, this one reflects a steadier and less school-shaped rhythm. It is about longer projects, production responsibility, and a deeper engineering identity that can later branch into writing, side projects, or future moves.

items: Continuation of the Verily internship into a full-time role
Represents the most up-to-date experience thread in the graph
A natural bridge back to the central bio node and present tense

adversarial-writing.title: Adversarial ML Writing Trail
adversarial-writing.subtitle: Zhihu articles, surveys, and dense notes
adversarial-writing.summary: A long writing trail on adversarial examples, robustness, and interpretability, spanning public explainers, research maps, and paper notes.

blocks.text: The old site held a large body of writing on adversarial machine learning. Some pieces explained ideas for a broad audience. Others worked more like study notes, mapping papers, concepts, and debates into something I could revisit later.
In retrospect, these pieces were less about publishing polished essays than about building a durable reading trail. Writing was a way to turn dense research into something that felt *navigable*.

items: Zhihu explainers on **adversarial examples**, adversarial training, and regularization
Long-form note collections such as *A Map of Adversarial Example Research*
Single-paper and dense-paper notes on robustness, defense, and interpretation

blocks.title: Writing function
blocks.text: A lot of this work sat between reading and research. The pieces were public enough to share, but they were also a way to keep an internal map of a field that could otherwise sprawl too quickly.

history-writing.title: History Writing
history-writing.subtitle: Essays from outside the technical track
history-writing.summary: A parallel writing thread focused on history rather than engineering, with essays on war, territory, and long arcs of political change.

blocks.text: This node keeps the graph from collapsing into only study and work. The old site also made room for essays driven by curiosity about history, geography, and the movement of states and ideas over time.

items: The Ripples of War
A history essay on the territorial changes of China
A quieter humanities thread alongside technical writing

northeast-2022.title: Northeast Corridor Trips
northeast-2022.subtitle: Cornell-year loops through NYC, Philly, Boston, and upstate
northeast-2022.summary: A cluster of 2022 trips radiating out from Ithaca during the Cornell period, mixing museums, city walks, nearby escapes, and the feeling of learning the U.S. Northeast through movement.

blocks.text: The old tripline files suggest this was less a single trip than a travel season: New York City in January, Philadelphia in February, Boston in June, and repeated returns to Ithaca and upstate New York through the rest of the year.

items: New York City, Jan. 21-23, 2022
Philadelphia, Feb. 25-28, 2022
Boston and whale watching, Jun. 24-28, 2022
Ithaca and New York upstate loops across 2022

items.alt: Haoyang in New York City during the Cornell period
items.caption: New York became one of the recurring anchors in that travel season.

puerto-rico-2022.title: Puerto Rico
puerto-rico-2022.subtitle: December 2022 island trip
puerto-rico-2022.summary: A late-2022 trip that feels like a marker between the Cornell chapter and what came next, with beaches, coastal stops, and a very different atmosphere from Ithaca.

blocks.text: The old tripline artwork turns this into a vivid node: Old San Juan, Punta Higuero Lighthouse, beaches, airport thresholds, and a compact trip at the end of 2022 that feels transitional in the larger story.

items: Old San Juan
Punta Higuero Lighthouse
Playa Escondida and other coastal stops

california-one-2022.title: California State Route 1
california-one-2022.subtitle: A coastal drive during the Verily summer
california-one-2022.summary: A Pacific coast travel node tied to the California internship period, with a scenic route through places like Half Moon Bay, Santa Cruz, Monterey, and 17-Mile Drive.

image.alt: Haoyang in Monterey during the Route 1 trip
image.caption: Monterey became the clearest visual memory in a day that started in the Bay and slowly tilted south.

blocks.text: Among the old travel artifacts, this one is especially visual. It reads more like a route sketch than a diary entry: coastlines, viewpoints, and a day-long line from the Bay toward Monterey during the [Verily internship](/nodes/verily-intern).
What stayed with me was not a single stop, but the feeling of the whole drive unfolding in sequence, almost like stepping through a living `storyboard` of California.

items: **Half Moon Bay** as the first clear transition out of the Bay rhythm
*Santa Cruz* as the midpoint where the coast started to feel more leisurely than logistical
**Monterey** as the emotional center of the trip
*17-Mile Drive* as the part that made the route feel intentionally scenic rather than merely efficient

blocks.title: Why it stayed
blocks.text: This was the first trip in California that felt fully inhabited rather than borrowed. It made the summer read less like a temporary relocation and more like a chapter.

items.alt: Haoyang in the Bay Area during summer 2022
items.caption: The Bay was the starting point before the drive stretched into a longer coastal day.

items.alt: Haoyang in Monterey during the Route 1 trip
items.caption: By Monterey the trip had slowed down enough to feel memorable rather than fast.

items.alt: Haoyang in Monterey during the Route 1 trip
